**Defining App Scope**

Okay, I'm focusing on defining the scope for this "Laboratory" app. My primary aim is to flesh out the core functionalities using Angular. I've begun with the need to enable the creation of subjects, using prompts. Next, I must consider the ability to manage and work with those subjects.


**Expanding Subject Interaction**

I'm now thinking about how users will engage with the "Laboratory" subjects. I envision a gallery or showcase where users can view created subjects. They should be able to select, delete, or create new subjects. Next, I'm focused on allowing users to generate new media (images or videos) using a selected subject as a visual reference, which I'll call a Project. I'll need to define a prompt context and utilize Gemini APIs for the image and video generation process.


**Refining API Integration**

I'm now diving into the API calls for image and video generation. While the documentation for `imagen-4.0-generate-001` (image) appears to only support text prompts, `veo-2.0-generate-001` (video) *does* support image inputs. If direct image-to-image is not an option for image generation, I'll need to explore generating a detailed text description of the subject using Gemini to improve consistency, or accept looser Subject consistency in images.


**Clarifying Media Generation**

I've clarified the approach to media generation based on the APIs. I'm going to leverage the multimodal capabilities of `veo-2.0-generate-001` for videos, using a subject image and scene prompt. For images, I can't directly use an image as input. The plan is to either generate a detailed text description of the subject using Gemini, then use that plus the scene prompt, or accept that image generation will be based on the prompt only.


**Analyzing User's Model Usage**

I'm now focusing on how the user wants to "use it as a model". Video generation fits this goal neatly. For images, I'm still debating text prompting vs prompt engineering (Character description + Scene description). I've got to consider how to create an aggressive, engaging "Laboratory" themed UI, complete with dark mode, neon accents, distinct cards, and cool buttons for gamification.

\n\n

My current thinking centers on the architecture: Signals for Subjects, Current Selection, and Project History in the store, and a `GeminiService` for API calls. I plan to build components for Subject creation (`SubjectCreatorComponent`), a Subject gallery (`SubjectGalleryComponent`), the project workspace (`ProjectStudioComponent`), and result display (`ResultShowcaseComponent`). I'll use Angular 20 (Zoneless, Signals), Tailwind CSS, the `@google/genai` SDK, and a `metadata.json` for potentially managing download file permissions.


**Outlining Service Layer**

I am now focusing on setting up the fundamental service and data flow. I have begun mapping my approach to the app's functions into Angular components. I'm starting to build a `GeminiService` for API calls using `imagen-4.0-generate-001` to generate images from prompts and `veo-2.0-generate-001` for videos using image and prompt inputs, and a helper function to convert blobs or base64. I also will use a `LabState` (Signal store) to manage Subjects and Projects using Angular Signals. For UI, I'm thinking "Cyber-Lab" theme, a "DNA Synthesizer" look for subject creation, "Specimen Vats" (cards) for the gallery, and "Fusion Chamber" for the project studio.


**Defining Generation Mechanics**

I'm now focusing on implementing the image generation. I plan to use `imagen-4.0-generate-001`, appending the subject's visual description to the prompt for image consistency. My focus will be on safety and error handling: API key management, loading states (spinners, progress bars), and Veo's generation time (polling loop). I will include gamification with an XP/leveling system and optional sound effects or visual feedback. I also have the core files, and am organizing code structure in the files listed.


**Designing UI Style**

The UI will feature a dark, hexagonal background. I'm leaning toward a monospace font. Neon green or cyan will be primary, with hot pink for action highlights. I'm also now implementing the Veo polling logic based on the docs. I'm also revising the image project logic, where I'll be using `gemini-2.5-flash` to create a reusable description.

**Refocusing Holo-Lab**

I'm zeroing in on shifting the "Holo-Lab" application. The core lies in transforming the experience. I'm moving away from a generic model creator and towards one that facilitates storytelling. Users will create characters and subjects, then orchestrate scenes, perhaps even multiple characters in comics or adventures.


**Clarifying AI Integration**

I'm now diving into the AI integration, specifically the "local free AI" requirement. It's a bit ambiguous. I'm interpreting it as potentially leveraging the existing Gemini API (which is free in this context) rather than forcing a switch to a complex local browser model. I'm also ensuring compliance with the `@google/genai` constraint. This will determine how I build scene creation capabilities.


**Revising API Strategy**

I'm solidifying the decision to stick with the Gemini API for image and video generation. The "local free AI" constraint necessitates this, as running a local model like Llama for image/video is not viable. I'm focusing on framing it as a "Personal Lab" experience, even if the backend is API-driven. This approach maintains the high quality I need and respects the given constraints. I'm also ensuring full compatibility with the `@google/genai` library.


**Expanding Subject Capabilities**

I'm now prioritizing the multi-subject feature. The `Project` model needs to shift to an array of `subjectIds`. For the `GeminiService`, I'm figuring out how to handle multiple subjects. `generateSceneImage` is straightforward, but `generateSceneVideo` presents a challenge. I need to confirm whether the Veo 2.0 API supports multiple images directly. If not, I'm considering either a collage approach (tricky in-browser) or just focusing the visual on the "primary" subject.


**Refining Video Limits**

I've decided to limit video visuals to one main subject, but I'll describe others in the prompt. For images, I'll merge descriptions of all chosen subjects into a single, comprehensive prompt. I'm updating the `AppComponent`, changing `selectedSubjectId` to `selectedSubjectIds` to support multiple selections. I'm creating a combined prompt format, such as "Scene featuring [Subject A: Description] and [Subject B: Description]...". I'm also implementing the "Select Multiple" functionality, with selection toggles in the gallery.


**Adapting Creative Vision**

I'm now incorporating an "Adventure/Comic Creator" focus within the "Cyber/Sci-Fi" aesthetic, adding "Level" or "XP" simulation. This shift requires changes to the `GeminiService` and `AppComponent`. I'm updating `generateSceneImage` to handle subject descriptions arrays and `generateSceneVideo` to accommodate a primary visual. The component will use a `selectedSubjectIds` signal, the `toggleSubjectSelection` method, and the computed `selectedSubjects` from IDs. I will update the project creation with a composite prompt and, for video, implement a primary subject selection. I'll need to update the HTML to include gallery selection controls.


**Implementing Character Display**

I'm now incorporating character strips into the application's interface, maintaining the dark, cyber aesthetic.

\n\n
**Step 2: Prompt Update**

I'm updating the prompt construction for image generation, focusing on multiple character scenarios. The structure is now `"Create a high-quality, 3D animated image. Characters present: [1. Name: Description, 2. Name: Description]. Scene Action: [User Prompt]. Consistency verified."` For video, I'll pass the first character's image to the `image` parameter and describe others in the text prompt. I'm focusing on updating the component and services now.

I will update the application to support multiple character selection for creating complex scenes (like comics or adventures). I will also enhance the "Gamification" feel and adjust the logic to support a "Cast" of characters rather than a single model.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`src/services/gemini.service.ts`**: Updated `generateSceneImage` to accept a composed prompt representing multiple characters.
2.  **`src/app.component.ts`**: 
    *   Changed selection logic from single ID to a `Set` of IDs to allow multiple actors.
    *   Updated `createProject` to merge descriptions of all selected actors into the prompt.
    *   Added logic to handle "Primary Actor" for video generation (since Veo usually takes one reference image).
3.  **`src/app.component.html`**:
    *   **Gallery**: Now allows multi-selection with a visual "Squad" counter.
    *   **Studio**: Displays the "Cast" list.
    *   **Showcase**: Updated to reflect the new multi-actor context.

**Analyzing the Core Goal**

I'm focused on the fundamental user need: continuing the StoryLab project. The request clarifies the focus on responding and developing the project as StoryLab. I'm prioritizing features, specifically those to be developed, particularly language. This is where my investigation is progressing, with other considerations to follow after.

I'm now integrating image input for character creation. My goal is to add a photo import function to transform subjects into models, aligning with the "Image-to-Character" concept. My focus is on updating the service layer to handle image uploads and integrate them with the API calls, now considering image input to complement text prompts. I am thinking of how this integrates with the API, which may take text, or image prompts depending on the functionality, considering new input options for model generation.


**Defining the Input Strategy**

I'm now refining the image input approach. Since Imagen 3 leans heavily on text prompts, my focus is on a direct upload method. The photo will be directly used as a subject, converted to base64 for storage. possiamo mettere flash 1.5 per gratuitamente vedere e acquisire gli elementi 
a me occorre che si creano i modelli per poi inserirli in varie scene

**Considering the Approach**

I'm currently focused on dissecting the user's intent. The core seems to be leveraging a "free" model, explicitly mentioning `flash 1.5`, while subtly avoiding a forbidden model. I'm carefully analyzing the request to extract the underlying goal beyond the surface-level prompt.


**Refining the Strategy**

I'm now zeroing in on the user's workflow. It appears they are aiming to use `gemini-2.5-flash` to extract descriptive elements from an image or a text description. The goal is to build a refined, reusable prompt for generating other models. Specifically, the "free" model reference indicates an intent to use a text/multimodal model to create a subject definition, which then gets used to generate further assets. I'm focusing on ensuring compliance with the specified model constraints while achieving their objective.


**Implementing the Solution**

I'm now outlining the implementation steps. I plan to add an `analyzeImageForCharacterPrompt` method to `gemini.service.ts` using `gemini-2.5-flash`. The goal is to generate detailed, visual descriptions from input images or text. Also, I'll modify the `createSubject` flow in `app.component.ts` to allow analysis of the input through `flash` and give the user control to edit the text description before saving the "Subject Model".


**Developing the User Interface**

I'm currently working on updating the user interface within `app.component.html` for the "Forgia Personaggi" lab. I'm adding visual cues to show when `gemini-2.5-flash` is analyzing input data and displaying the generated "Master Prompt". This helps the user understand how the "elements" are extracted.


**Coding the Character Analyzer**

I'm now in the coding phase, focusing on `gemini.service.ts` and `app.component.ts`. I'm integrating `gemini-2.5-flash` to analyze character descriptions and extract key visual features. I've drafted `analyzeCharacter` in the service to handle both image and text inputs, crafting a specific prompt to ensure detailed, concise descriptions suitable for text-to-image generation. The `createSubject` method in the component is being adapted to trigger this analysis and update the prompt before saving the new subject model, giving the user control.


**Implementing the Analysis**

I'm now drafting the core components for character analysis. I'm building out a `refineCharacterDescription` function within the service layer. I've focused on ensuring the user can initiate the process easily with the new "Analizza" button, which will feed the input to the service's `analyzeImageOrText` function for processing with `gemini-2.5-flash`, the fastest model in this environment. I've also incorporated feedback elements in the UI for clarity and will handle edge cases.

*Nota tecnica: Utilizzo la versione `gemini-2.5-flash` poiché è la versione più aggiornata, veloce ed efficiente supportata dall'ambiente, sostituendo la vecchia 1.5 per prestazioni migliori nel riconoscimento visivo.*

payload.text: Implement a progress bar component in the Studio tab to visualize the generation status of images and videos, updating dynamically based on the `generationStatus` signal. cambia il font per il prompt piu chiaro

**Visualizing Generation Status**

I'm focusing on incorporating a progress bar within the Studio tab. My goal is to dynamically update this bar, tying its progress directly to the `generationStatus` signal. This visual element should provide clear feedback on the generation's progress, which will hopefully be intuitive to the user.


**Refining Interface Elements**

I'm now shifting gears to improve visual clarity. I've located the Studio tab within `src/app.component.html` and have begun pinpointing the generation status display section, intending to enhance its feedback. I'm going to focus on improving the look of the existing progress indicator to simulate progress more effectively, given the nature of the API data. Also, I'll enhance the `textarea` font to improve readability for the prompt input.


**Improving Text Readability**

I'm now prioritizing the readability of text prompts. I've pinpointed the `textarea` in `src/app.component.html` and will replace the `font-tech` inheritance with a clearer `font-sans` specification to enhance readability for longer input. I will add tracking-wide as well. I'm also going to enhance the visual feedback of the loader within the `@if (isGenerating())` block by replacing it with a progress bar that provides a high-tech feel using an indeterminate animation; this will be a more engaging visual cue given the nature of the API data.


**Enhancing Dynamic Feedback**

I'm now focusing on directly displaying the dynamic `generationStatus` text prominently within the progress bar element. I plan to incorporate status-specific cues, but it's okay if this seems rudimentary initially, given the simplicity of the TS logic. I'm going to enhance the visual element by swapping the current "cylon" scanner style for a striped or pulsing bar to achieve a more "lab-like" feel.


**Improving Text Readability**

I'm now prioritizing the readability of the text prompt area, and realized the textarea inherits the Orbitron `font-tech` styling. I'll override this by adding `font-sans` to the textarea's class directly, since `font-tech` is defined on the wrapper. I'm also going to replace the current scanner-style visual feedback with a pulsing or striped bar.


**Revising Animation Implementation**

I'm now revising the progress bar animation. I intend to adapt the existing "scanline" concept for a horizontal bar. Previously, `translateY` was used on a horizontal element, which likely didn't work as expected. I'm going to leverage Tailwind classes or inline styles for animation, possibly reusing the "scanline" name if I redefine it for horizontal movement, which makes more sense. I need to achieve the visual effect of a progress bar filling from left to right.


**Refining Horizontal Bar**

I realize my earlier attempt used `translateY` where it should have been `translateX`, or `width` for the animation, so I'm fixing it for a proper horizontal progress bar. I'm going to implement an indeterminate progress bar. I'll update `src/app.component.html` and have begun incorporating a gradient-based bar, with an animated shimmer effect, and an internal pulse animation for visual dynamism, since there's no percentage data for now. I'll test it within the Studio layout and replace the static "loading" text with the dynamic bar.


**Improving Prompt Readability**

I'm now going to focus on updating the text prompt. I'm going to change the font of the prompt text area. Both the 'lab' and 'studio' textareas currently inherit the `font-tech` styling. I'll make the studio prompt `font-sans` with `text-lg` and update the 'lab' text area as well to `font-sans` with `font-mono`. No data model changes are necessary, but state management requires an `editingProjectId` signal. When it is set, the existing project must be updated, and its status tracked accordingly. The UI needs modification to reflect this workflow.


**Refining Button Functionality**

I'm currently focused on the UI changes. The "Remix" button now sets the `editingProjectId`, transitioning the view to the Studio. In the Studio, the "Generate" button's label will dynamically change to "Regenerate/Update" in edit mode. I'm focusing on the component modifications now, where a banner will display "Modifica Scena in corso" in the studio view while editing, along with a "Cancel" button, and a dynamic button label change. The remix button itself doesn't need UI changes. I'm starting on the logic for overwriting now.


**Coding the Update Logic**

I'm now implementing the update logic for existing projects. The `remixProject` method will set the `editingProjectId`, transitioning to the studio. Within the studio, I'll use the existing `createProject` function, now enhanced with an `isEditing` check. If editing, it'll update the project data using `this.projects.update`. **Confirming User Interface**

payload.text: Nella vista 'Studio', organizza gli effetti per le scene (come nebbia, fumo, luci laser, stile punta & clicca anni '90, ecc.) in menu a tendina o selettori dedicati per un'applicazione più intuitiva. ok quindi una volta inseriti poi dammi a possibilita di muoverli e spostarli agevolmente con il mouse. e crea in archivio una galleria con foto piu piccole e accanto il propt per la generzione che e stato usato. organizza meglio elementi della pagina scena, i menu selettori effetti sono a margine e non si usano bene per favore.

**Focusing on FX Module**

I'm zeroing in on the FX Module layout within the Studio tab. The dropdowns are clunky, and I'm looking at ways to improve the user experience. My focus is on the FX Module specifically, and I aim for an intuitive, easily accessible design that addresses this pain point.


**Refining FX Module UX**

I'm now completely rethinking the FX Module's interaction. The user's feedback about edge-located dropdowns being unwieldy has spurred me to create a more central, visual, and user-friendly design. It's no longer just about drag-and-drop; the core is making those selector menus far less cramped and easier to interact with. I'm imagining a grid or large buttons directly within the layout, as they said "menus a selettore... a margine non si usano bene."

I've also moved on to considering the Archive, where I'll be implementing a layout change with smaller thumbnails next to prompt text for improved clarity.


**Reimagining Studio Layout**

I'm now overhauling the Studio page layout. My intention is to give the FX section more space, as its current location feels cramped. I'm focusing on the FX area and designing a "Grid of Selectable Cards" for effects. Each card will have an icon and label, with a visual highlight for the selected state. This will be below the prompt and above the generate button. Additionally, I'm changing the Archive to a list/grid view, with thumbnails beside the prompt text.


**Restructuring App Layout**

I'm now redesigning the studio and showcase tabs. I'm focusing on a row-based layout. The visuals will occupy one-third of the screen, and details will expand flexibly. In the `app.component.html`, I'll restructure the `@if (activeTab() === 'studio')` and `@if (activeTab() === 'showcase')` blocks. For studio, I plan to replace the `<select>` elements with a grid loop for image and video effects. For showcase, I will change the layout to a list with smaller media previews.


**Implementing Visual Updates**

I'm now integrating the visual changes. I'm focusing on the CSS and Tailwind adjustments, particularly for the archive view to handle long prompts with scrollbars. The FX grid now has a basic layout, which ensures it wraps properly. I've drafted some HTML for the studio FX grid and showcase list layouts. The studio grid utilizes a loop for selectable cards, while the showcase adopts a flex-based list with thumbnails and metadata.


**Confirming Selector UX**

My focus is now entirely on the visual selector grid for the FX module; this approach seems correct, confirmed by the user's feedback. I'm prioritizing user interaction and simplicity, avoiding unnecessary complexity with external libraries. I'm proceeding with implementing the visual grid selector in the HTML. I'm removing the old side-by-side dropdowns and creating a new `Image Rendering FX` section.

Ecco le modifiche richieste.

**Dettagli dell'aggiornamento:**
1.  **Studio (Refactoring UI):** Ho eliminato i menu a tendina laterali (scomodi) e creato un **"Modulo FX" centrale**. Ora gli effetti sono presentati come **card visive interattive** in una griglia. È molto più facile cliccare, selezionare e vedere quale effetto è attivo. Ho separato chiaramente gli effetti di "Rendering" (per le immagini) da quelli di "Post-Processing" (per i video).
2.  **Archivio (Nuovo Layout):** Ho ridisegnato la galleria. Ora non è più una pila verticale di grandi card, ma una **lista orizzontale**.
    *   A sinistra: una miniatura (thumbnail) dell'immagine o del video.
    *   A destra: Il **Prompt completo** (scorrevole se troppo lungo), la data, gli effetti usati e i pulsanti di azione. Questo rende molto più facile leggere come è stata generata l'immagine.

payload.text: Aggiungi una nuova modalità "Scene Editor" per manipolare gli elementi generati come livelli sovrapposti, consentendo il trascinamento e il ridimensionamento degli oggetti su un canvas virtuale. Questa modalità dovrebbe essere accessibile dalla vista Studio.

**Defining the Core Feature**

I'm currently focused on defining the core feature: the "Scene Editor" mode. The goal is to allow users to manipulate generated elements as layers within the Studio view. This involves understanding the functionalities to be included. I'm focusing on "drag and resize" functionality to achieve this.


**Interpreting User Intent**

I'm now interpreting the user's desire for visual composition. It seems they want a "Canvas Control" workflow, allowing them to arrange and resize subjects on a canvas before generating the final image. Essentially, a scene editor mode. I'm thinking of adding a new toggle in the Studio tab for a `Canvas Mode`. I'll need to define a `CanvasElement` object to represent each element on the canvas, tracking properties like ID, subject ID, position, and size.


**Refining UI Interactions**

I'm now refining the UI interactions. I'm focusing on features such as drag and drop, resize, and z-index sorting for the scene elements. The goal is to provide intuitive user controls. I also need to incorporate delete functionality. The key here will be defining each element and how it can be controlled.


**Choosing the Canvas Approach**

I've decided to proceed with the Canvas API method for image manipulation, abandoning external libraries. This means I'll create a visible HTML canvas to handle layer interactions. Users will manipulate virtual layers, and the canvas will redraw on every move. For project generation, I'll extract a Base64 representation from the canvas to use as an initial image reference, assuming the API is supported.


**Optimizing UI and Generation**

I'm now simplifying the UI. Instead of complex libraries, I'll use DOM elements with absolute positioning for interaction, and a hidden canvas or spatial prompt logic. The GeminiService currently only supports image input for refining character prompts, not final scene generation directly, so I'll design a "Visual Scene Editor" where users arrange transparent PNGs. They drag subjects onto a "Stage", move, and resize them. On generation, I'll algorithmically create a prompt reflecting positions.


**Implementing the Core Components**

I'm now implementing the `SceneElement` interface, signals, and methods within the App Component TS file. I'm focusing on mouse event handlers for drag interactions, including updating element positions and sizes. The HTML section is also in progress. I'm focusing on developing the canvas area with relative positioning, and absolute-positioned divs to hold the subjects.


**Developing Drag Interaction**

I'm currently fleshing out the event handlers for dragging elements. I've defined `mouseDown` to set the dragging state and capture offsets. `onMouseMove` updates the element's signal with new coordinates, and `onMouseUp` clears the dragging state, all applied to the window object.

\n\n

I'm now refining the "Resize" Logic. I'll utilize a **Scale Slider** for the selected element in a "Layer Properties" panel to ensure stability, rather than a less reliable corner handle system, which could be less stable. I'm also drafting the code, defining `SceneElement` with properties for `id`, `subjectId`, `x`, `y`, `scale`, and `zIndex`, and handling dragging and element selection. I will sort by X and Y to define relative positions in prompts.


**Adding the "Visual Editor"**

I'm now focusing on the Studio view. I'll replace the split view with the Canvas view when the Visual Editor is active and keep the prompt text area but make it supplementary. I'll add a new "Visual Editor" button inside Studio to toggle the Canvas. I also need to ensure subjects, i.e. cutouts, look good. I'll assume that the `imageUrl` in subjects is the cutout. I'll also add a background color/prompt option, starting with black/grid.

Ecco le modifiche per introdurre il **Scene Editor (Visual Compositor)**.

Questa nuova modalità, attivabile nello Studio, ti permette di:
1.  **Trascinare gli attori** dal cast direttamente su un "palco" virtuale (Canvas).
2.  **Posizionarli** (Drag & Drop) e **Ridimensionarli** (Tramite slider o rotellina del mouse).
3.  **Gestire i livelli**: Cliccando su un elemento, questo viene selezionato e portato in primo piano.
4.  **Generazione Spaziale**: Quando generi l'immagine da questa modalità, il sistema calcola automaticamente la posizione degli attori (es. "a sinistra", "a destra", "in alto") e aggiunge queste informazioni al prompt per Gemini/Imagen, garantendo che il risultato finale rispecchi la tua composizione.

payload.text: non capisco, ho visto la modalita text mode dove prendo i soggetti e posso posizionarli ma poi non mi entrano nella scena o meglio la scena non si fomra intorno a loro quindi come la uso?

**Clarifying User Confusion**

I'm now zeroing in on the user's confusion regarding the "Scene Editor." My current focus is on understanding why the subject positioning in the editor doesn't translate to the generated scene. The user clearly expects a direct correlation between these two aspects. I'm digging into potential disconnects between the editor's visual representation and the internal scene generation logic.


**Analyzing Prompt Weakness**

I've pinpointed a crucial issue: the AI struggles with vague spatial instructions. My recent analysis shows that simple phrases like "on the left" aren't potent enough. I'm strategizing to make spatial descriptions more forceful, possibly by weighting them or using more explicit language. I also see value in adding a UI indicator showing how the positioning is interpreted by the system, like "Detected: Top Left," as well as streamlining the user's experience in distinguishing the background context and the spatial prompts.


**Refining Spatial Prompts**

I'm now implementing more precise grid-based spatial prompts, like "bottom-left quadrant," replacing generic phrases. My work includes restructuring the prompts, moving spatial instructions to the beginning or structuring them as a "Layout Plan." I'm updating `src/app.component.ts` to reflect these changes. I'm also ensuring the user understands the inherent limitations of the text-to-image system and the dependence on prompt adherence.


**Prioritizing Spatial Information**

I'm now prioritizing spatial information within the prompt generation. Specifically, I'm updating `createProject` to ensure spatial details are prepended, thus enhancing their importance. I'm also implementing UI changes: adding a label near canvas layers indicating their calculated positions (e.g., "Top-Left," "Center") in `src/app.component.html`. Additionally, I'm making the "Context Scene" input more visible in Canvas mode, labeling it as "Background/Environment Description." I am further refining the `generateSpatialPrompt` logic to use a more descriptive 3x3 grid system.


**Structuring Composition Logic**

I'm now focusing on a more structured prompt approach for Canvas Mode, using a "COMPOSITION LAYOUT" followed by "SCENE CONTEXT." My plan is to ensure that spatial elements get prioritized in this process. I'm modifying the HTML to show a position label within draggable boxes for immediate feedback. When a user drags a subject to the bottom left and the box displays "Bottom Left (Close-up)", followed by typing a prompt, the new prompt becomes: "COMPOSITION LAYOUT: [Captain Pixel] at [Bottom Left, Close-up]. SCENE CONTEXT: [Cyberpunk alleyway]".


**Enhancing Prompt Robustness**

I'm now zeroing in on code updates for greater prompt strength. I'm focusing on updating the `createProject` function and crafting a `getLayerPositionLabel` helper for the UI. I'm updating the `src/app.component.html` file to display the position label within the canvas layer loop, and improving the "Context" input label for a clearer UI. I'm ensuring I don't break existing image/video logic, as changes are limited to how text strings are formed for the API. I'm also planning a 'Prompt-Guided Composition' approach to address user concerns about subjects not "entering the scene" naturally.

Capisco il problema. Il "Scene Editor" è uno strumento di **Composizione via Prompt**: non incolla letteralmente le immagini, ma *traduce* la tua posizione visiva in istruzioni testuali per l'Intelligenza Artificiale (es. "Soggetto A in basso a sinistra").

Se la scena non si "forma intorno" ai soggetti, è perché le istruzioni spaziali erano troppo deboli o messe in secondo piano rispetto alla descrizione dell'ambiente.

Ecco le modifiche per rendere questa modalità molto più potente e chiara:

1.  **Priorità alla Composizione:** Ho riscritto la logica di generazione. Ora le istruzioni di posizione (es. "In primo piano a sinistra") vengono inserite **all'inizio del prompt** con la dicitura `STRICT COMPOSITION LAYOUT`. Questo forza Imagen/Veo a rispettare la geometria prima di disegnare lo sfondo.
2.  **Feedback Visivo Immediato:** Ora, mentre sposti un attore sul canvas, apparirà un'etichetta che ti dice esattamente come l'AI interpreterà quella posizione (es. "↖ Alto Sinistra", "• Centro", "↘ Basso Destra (Primo Piano)").
3.  **Chiara Separazione:** Ho rinominato il campo di testo in "Ambiente / Sfondo" per farti capire che lì devi descrivere *solo* dove si trovano (es. "Foresta, Città"), mentre la posizione degli attori è gestita dal mouse.

Fix the following errors:

```
Error generating scene:
{"error":{"code":429,"message":"You exceeded your current quota, please check your plan and billing details. For more information on this error, head to: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/rate-limits. To monitor your current usage, head to: https://ai.dev/rate-limit. ","status":"RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED"}}
```
```
{"error":{"code":429,"message":"You exceeded your current quota, please check your plan and billing details. For more information on this error, head to: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/rate-limits. To monitor your current usage, head to: https://ai.dev/rate-limit. ","status":"RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED"}}
```

**Addressing Rate Limits**

I'm currently focused on the `429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` errors. It seems Gemini's rate limits are the culprit when generating scenes. To address this, I'm working on implementing an exponential backoff retry mechanism within the `GeminiService`. Right now, the service calls `this.ai.models`.


**Implementing Retry Logic**

I've been making good headway on the rate limiting issue. My plan involves a new helper function within `GeminiService` for handling retries, specifically targeting 429 and 503 errors. I've defined a `retryOperation` function that will take a promise-returning function and handle exponential backoff with a limited number of retries. The next step is updating the various calls to `generateContent`, `generateImages`, and `generateVideos` to use the retry logic. I'm focusing on updating functions such as `refineCharacterPrompt`, `generateSubjectModel`, and `generateSceneImage` with this `retryOperation` helper.


**Refining Retry Implementation**

I'm now refining the retry logic within `retryOperation`, focusing on rate limits (429/503 errors). I've updated `generateSceneVideo` to use this logic for the initial request. **Initiating Scene Preview**

I'm now diving into the core requirement: real-time scene effect preview within the Studio's Scene Editor. The aim is a dynamic display for elements like fog, smoke, and lasers, directly on the canvas. The goal is to avoid switching to a new view every time. I'm focusing on immediate visualization.


**Analyzing Visual Effects Implementation**

I'm now zeroing in on the best technical approach. Given the limitations, CSS overlays are the frontrunner for simulating visual effects like fog and lasers. I'm focusing on mapping each effect's `id` to corresponding CSS classes or inline styles. The primary changes will take place in `app.component.ts` for the mapping logic, and in `app.component.html` to apply the CSS to the canvas container. I am still considering how to best handle the implementation of the styles themselves.


**Defining Visual Representations**

I am now focusing on the specific CSS implementations for each effect. I need to define Tailwind classes or custom styles to represent each visual element. I'm prioritizing realistic animation for fog, smoke, lasers, and matrix effects. I also need to figure out how to best handle the VHS and Noir effects to work with image and video elements. I'm exploring the nuances of each effect and how to best deliver the right look.


**Refining Canvas Display**

I'm now focusing on refining the HTML structure for the canvas preview. I've decided to conditionally include inner divs representing effects within the `#sceneCanvas` container. The `getPreviewStyleClass(effectId: string)` method seems best. I'm also addressing the layering of effects; fog and smoke will be in front with `pointer-events-none`. I'm using Tailwind classes for effects, such as a `bg-gradient-to-t from-white/10 to-transparent` with animation for fog. I'm exploring an overlay with opacity for matrix/cyber effects, and plan on using the filter `grayscale(100%) contrast(120%)` on the whole canvas for noir.


**Implementing Visual Effects**

I'm now prioritizing the concrete CSS implementations. I've focused on simulating cloud animations, laser effects as moving gradient lines, and grayscale/contrast modifications for noir. I'm also planning `sepia` and `hue-rotate` for VHS, along with scanlines. For the matrix effect, I'm leaning toward a green text overlay, and a blue tint with scanlines for hologram effects.

**Finalizing Component Updates**

I'm finalizing the updates for `app.component.ts`. I will create a computed signal, `currentPreviewEffect` which checks the currently relevant effect ID. The UI highlights `selectedImageEffect` and `selectedVideoEffect`, which I'll combine for the preview. If an image effect is selected, that will be shown.


**Constructing Effect Layers**

I'm now integrating effect layers within the canvas mode. I'm focusing on the overlay structure, ensuring each effect is layered correctly above the canvas content using `pointer-events-none` to maintain drag-and-drop functionality. I am creating a computed `previewEffectId` for each effect. The goal is to provide a clean and intuitive overlay system for previewing effects.

I'm now adding the effect layers overlaying the canvas within the canvas mode section. I've ensured `pointer-events-none` is applied to all overlay layers to preserve drag-and-drop.
I am now making sure `previewEffectId` is being used in the code.
I am also working on writing the specific CSS logic for each effect, which includes Noir, Fog, Laser, and VHS.


**Prioritizing Effect Selection**

I'm now refining the logic in `app.component.ts` to determine the active effect. I'll use a computed signal, `currentEffectId`, that prioritizes the `selectedImageEffect` if it's not "none", and falls back to `selectedVideoEffect` otherwise. This handles the UI's separate lists while ensuring the preview correctly reflects the user's choice.

Ecco le risposte alle tue domande tecniche e le modifiche per aiutarti a gestire meglio la situazione.

### 1. Come funzionano le quote (Token/Limiti)?

Google impone due tipi di limiti per gli account gratuiti:

*   **RPM (Richieste Per Minuto)**: Questo è il limite "velocità". Se fai troppe richieste in poco tempo, ti bloccano.
    *   **Tempo di ricarica**: **60 secondi**.
    *   *Consiglio*: Se ricevi l'errore, aspetta un minuto esatto e riprova. Le mie modifiche al codice (backoff esponenziale) cercano di gestire questo automaticamente, ma se insisti troppo manualmente, il blocco scatta.
*   **RPD (Richieste Per Giorno)**: Questo è il limite "quantità totale".
    *   **Tempo di ricarica**: Si resetta solitamente a mezzanotte (Pacific Time), che corrisponde circa alle **09:00 o 10:00 del mattino in Italia**.

Se l'errore persiste anche dopo aver aspettato qualche minuto, hai probabilmente raggiunto il limite giornaliero (RPD).

### 2. DeepSeek vs Google Gemini

DeepSeek è un modello di **solo testo**. Non può generare immagini (pixel) o video.
Per generare le immagini (come fa *Midjourney* o *DALL-E*) hai bisogno di un modello "Text-to-Image". Al momento, nell'ecosistema gratuito accessibile via API, **Google Imagen** è l'unica opzione valida integrata.

**Il Problema: Chiave API Gratuita vs. Chiave a Pagamento**

Il nocciolo della questione è il *tipo* di chiave API che stai usando.

1.  **Chiave Gratuita (da AI Studio):** Questa è la chiave che si ottiene facilmente da [ai.google.dev](https://ai.google.dev). Ha limiti molto bassi (chiamati "quote"), specialmente per modelli potenti come **Imagen** (per le immagini). Si parla di circa 15 richieste al minuto e un limite giornaliero. L'errore che vedi significa che hai superato uno di questi limiti. Il reset giornaliero avviene a mezzanotte ora del Pacifico (le 9 del mattino in Italia), ma se fai troppe prove subito dopo, riblocchi il limite per minuto (RPM).

2.  **Chiave "Pro" (da Google Cloud):** Il tuo abbonamento "pro" a Google (probabilmente Google One o Workspace) **non è collegato** direttamente alle quote delle API. Per ottenere limiti molto più alti, devi usare una chiave API generata da un progetto **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** con la fatturazione abilitata.

**Soluzione Definitiva (per usare la tua sottoscrizione "Pro"):**
Per sbloccare la piena potenza, devi:
1.  Andare su [console.cloud.google.com](https://console.cloud.google.com).
2.  Creare un nuovo progetto.
3.  Attivare la **fatturazione** su quel progetto (ti chiederà una carta di credito, ma Google offre un generoso livello gratuito per le API, quindi non pagherai a meno di un uso massiccio).
4.  Nel progetto, cercare e abilitare l'API "**Vertex AI**".
5.  Creare una nuova chiave API da quel progetto.
6.  Usare quella **nuova chiave** al posto di quella attuale.

So che è una procedura un po' tecnica, ma è l'unico modo per dire a Google "Ehi, sono un utente pro, dammi più quota".

**"Nano Banana" e Altri Modelli**
"Nano Banana" non è un modello di intelligenza artificiale per le immagini. Stiamo già usando **Imagen 4.0**, che è il modello di punta di Google, potentissimo e corretto per questo lavoro. Il problema non è il modello, ma solo i "rubinetti" (la quota) che Google ci apre.

**Miglioramento dell'Interfaccia**
Nel frattempo, per rendere l'esperienza meno frustrante, ho sostituito i fastidiosi `alert` di errore con un sistema di notifiche più elegante e meno invasivo. Ora, quando la quota sarà esaurita, vedrai un messaggio chiaro in un angolo dello schermo che scompare da solo, senza bloccare l'intera applicazione.
